summary: Before I was drafted in 1941, I was living in Chicago with my parents. I worked full-time as an assistant theater manager there. It was a different life compared to the Army, starting work at noon instead of waking up early. Chicago was home during those pre-service days and the place I returned to during furloughs.

1941 was the year I was drafted into the United States Army at the age of 25. It was a significant turning point, moving from civilian life in Chicago to military service. That year marked the beginning of my basic training at Camp Pickett and the start of my Army career.

Camp Pickett in Virginia was where I went for my basic training after being drafted in 1941. The training lasted about 16 weeks, and it was a rigorous introduction to military life, including calisthenics, rifle training, and learning the basics. I was a Private then and later stayed on to help supervise new inductees there.

Virginia was the state where Camp Pickett was located, and that's where I spent my initial Army training. It was quite a change from my life in Chicago, waking up early and living in barracks. I stayed in Virginia for about six months, including my time helping to train new soldiers.

Sergeant Shaughnessy was my drill sergeant during basic training at Camp Pickett. He was a fine young man who treated all of us equally and put us through the same maneuvers. I remember him as a fair leader who helped introduce me to Army life.

After basic training, I was retained at Fort Pickett initially, but later I was at Fort Meade following my commission as a Second Lieutenant. At Fort Meade, I was involved in training duties, supervising groups of soldiers. It was part of my early leadership experience in the Army.

I attended Officer Candidate School at Fort Barkeley, Texas, after about six months of basic training and supervising new recruits. The school lasted about thirteen weeks and included both classroom instruction and field exercises to prepare us for leadership roles. Graduating from OCS, I was commissioned as a Second Lieutenant.

Fort Barkley in Texas was where I went for Officer Candidate School after my initial training at Camp Pickett. I traveled there by troop train and spent about thirteen weeks learning the responsibilities of an officer. After graduation, I stayed on at Fort Barkley to help with training other candidates.

Texas was the state where Fort Barkley is located, and it was where I went for my officer training. Traveling there by troop train was a long trip, but once at Fort Barkley, I underwent rigorous training for thirteen weeks. Texas became an important place in my military career as the site of my commissioning.

After completing Officer Candidate School at Fort Barkley, I was commissioned as a Second Lieutenant. This rank marked my transition from enlisted man to officer and gave me leadership responsibilities. I held this rank as I continued training duties and later when I was deployed overseas.

About three or four months after commissioning, I was shipped overseas to North Africa, specifically near Algiers. I spent six to eight months there as part of the Army's operations during the war. It was my first experience in a combat zone and a significant step in my military service.

Algiers was the area in North Africa where I was stationed after leaving the United States. I arrived there by ship after a fourteen-day voyage through the Mediterranean, including passing the Strait of Gibraltar. Algiers was my first overseas posting during World War II.

After serving several months in North Africa, I was shipped to Italy where I continued my military service. Italy was part of the European theater where I experienced more direct involvement in the war. My time there followed my initial deployment in North Africa.

During my voyage to North Africa, our ship passed through the Strait of Gibraltar, a very narrow passage between Europe and Africa. It was a tense moment because the area could have been patrolled by German forces, but thankfully it was clear. Passing through there was a memorable part of the journey.

My ship sailed through the Mediterranean Sea on the way to North Africa. The fourteen-day trip was mostly uneventful and smooth, with troops living in hammocks aboard the converted ship. The Mediterranean was the body of water connecting us to the European and African theaters of war.

We disembarked at Bizerte, which is located at the far end of North Africa. I believe the name might have changed since then, but it was our entry point into the African theater. Bizerte was an important staging area for troops like me arriving overseas.

I served in the United States Army during World War II, starting from my draft in 1941. My journey took me from basic training at Camp Pickett to Officer Candidate School at Fort Barkley, and then overseas to North Africa and Italy. Throughout, I held roles from Private to Second Lieutenant, participating in training and combat operations.
